aliases: 云吞面 (Wán Tūn Miàn / Wonton Mee)

chinese: 云吞面 (Wán Tūn Miàn / Wonton Mee)

region_of_origin: Singapore (Cantonese origin, adapted for Singapore hawker context)
food_subgroup: Noodle dish — dry tossed thin egg noodles (mee kia) with char siu, wontons, and choy sum

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Wonton Mee. FOODON_03315393 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0DGM is the closest FoodEx2 parent for noodle dishes; no specific code exists for Wonton Mee in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Wonton Mee or equivalent Singapore hawker noodle dish.

allergens: gluten (wheat noodles, oyster sauce, soy sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of Wonton Mee Dry weighs approximately 380g and comprises thin egg noodles (~120g), char siu (~60g), three to four wontons (~80g), choy sum (~60g), and sauce coating (oyster sauce, dark soy, lard glaze ~60g combined).

nutrient_source.title: Wonton Mee — HPB Singapore Foodo / Healthhub.sg
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database lists Wonton Mee as a named dish entry. Per-100g values are taken directly from the HPB record. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, vitamins) are consistent with HPB Singapore published data for this dish category and are retained from the verified input record at confidence 1.0.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Asian Noodles — Mee Kia (thin egg noodles, kansui-processed); analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for egg noodles and peer-reviewed literature on alkaline-processed wheat noodles
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Singapore mee kia (thin kansui egg noodles) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~55 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: instant noodles and egg noodles (GI 40–60 range) in the Sydney GI Database, adjusted upward slightly for the thin-cut format and downward for kansui alkaline processing which increases protein cross-linking and reduces starch gelatinisation rate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 62g) / 100 = 34.1. Confidence 0.9. GI 55 is classified as 'low' per standard WHO/FAO classification (low GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~1,050mg per plate from oyster sauce, dark soy, char siu marinade, and wonton skin)
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should request 'less sauce' (少酱) to reduce sodium by approximately 200–300mg, skip the accompanying wonton broth (~150–200mg additional sodium), and consider requesting less char siu to further reduce sodium by ~160mg, targeting a total plate sodium of 700–900mg for an occasional hawker meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~55) but high glycemic load per serving (~34.1) due to the substantial noodle portion (~120g cooked mee kia)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that despite a low GI of ~55 (lower than standard wheat noodles due to kansui alkaline processing), the large carbohydrate load (~62g per plate) produces a high glycemic load of ~34.1; portion control — requesting a smaller noodle portion — is the primary strategy, while the protein from char siu and wontons and the fat from lard provide modest postprandial glucose moderation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lard glaze and char siu (~4.6g per plate), combined with high sodium (~1,050mg) and dietary cholesterol (~171mg per plate)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request 'no lard' (无猪油) to reduce saturated fat by approximately 3–4g per plate, limit char siu portion to reduce both saturated fat and sodium, and skip the wonton broth to minimise total sodium intake; the dish is acceptable occasionally within a heart-healthy diet if these modifications are applied.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from lard and char siu; high sodium relevant for patients on corticosteroids; choy sum contributes Vitamin C (~9.5mg per plate) and folate
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients on corticosteroids should monitor sodium intake (~1,050mg per plate); requesting 'no lard' and 'less sauce' reduces both saturated fat and sodium, while the choy sum component provides anti-inflammatory micronutrients including Vitamin C and folate.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wonton Mee Dry (云吞面干捞, Wàntūn miàn gān lāo) is a classic Singapore hawker dish of Cantonese origin, adapted from Hong Kong-style wonton noodles into the Singapore dry-toss format. A standard plate (~380g) consists of thin, springy alkaline egg noodles (mee kia) boiled and tossed in a coating of oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, and lard, topped with sliced char siu (Chinese BBQ pork loin), three to four plump pork-and-prawn wontons, and blanched choy sum (Chinese flowering cabbage). A small bowl of clear wonton broth is served alongside. The noodles are made with kansui (lye water / sodium carbonate solution), which gives them their characteristic yellow-green colour, firm springy bite, and slightly alkaline flavour, and also moderates their glycemic index to approximately 55 (low) compared to standard wheat noodles. Per plate (~380g): approximately 500 kcal, 18g protein, 15g fat (4.6g saturated), 62g carbohydrate, 3.1g dietary fibre, and ~1,050mg sodium. The dish is dairy-free but contains gluten, egg, shellfish (prawns), and soy. Key clinical considerations: sodium is high (~1,050mg per plate) from oyster sauce, dark soy, and char siu marinade — requesting 'less sauce' (少酱) is the primary sodium-reduction strategy; glycemic load is high (~34.1) despite low GI, due to the large noodle portion; lard contributes approximately 3–4g saturated fat and can be omitted on request ('no lard' / 无猪油). The dish is one of the most enduring Cantonese-influenced hawker foods in Singapore and is available at breakfast, lunch, and dinner at hawker centres and coffee shops island-wide.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (verified, confidence 1.0 per input record). GI estimated at medium confidence (0.9) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for egg noodles with documented kansui adjustment. All mandatory multilingual names present. Ontology anchors are defensible closest-parent matches with honest gap notes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Singapore Wonton Mee Dry is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite hawker dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore verified input record). GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (egg noodle category) adjusted for kansui alkaline processing; no direct GI measurement for Singapore mee kia exists in the Sydney database as of April 2026. GL per serving = (55 × 62g carbohydrate per 380g plate) / 100 = 34.1. Two default portions are noted in the source record (340g and 380g); 380g is used as the primary serving size as it matches the dish description. GI 55 is classified as 'low' per standard WHO/FAO classification (low GI ≤ 55). Hindi alias 'वोंटन मी (सूखा)' is a transliteration approximation; no established standardised Hindi culinary term exists for this dish.
